speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 3, March 18, 1997, interview with Esther Gever. You were in Poland at your paternal grandparents home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We met them. They were wonderful. My grandfather was a very pious Jew. And really, he was the only man I ever met that was so devoted to God with heart and soul. And in the worst of times, he never doubted. He was-- he had faith. And my grandmother was a very smart lady. I don't think she was exactly like my grandfather. She was religious. But I think she would have bent some rules if it was up to her. But she was in so in awe of her husband and lived for him and took care of him that she did everything that was expected of her. And she was a very loving mother, a very sacrificing mother, a mother that never needed anything, only for her children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My aunt was a businesswoman. She had several girls. She was a seamstress, a designer seamstress. She used to design clothes. And, and, and they came from all the towns around there for dowry outfits and things like that to my aunt. She was famous. She was one of the best. And so she was in business. She was earning money. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't know what my uncle did. I have absolutely no idea. I don't remember. But they were very nice family. My cousin was lovely, very warm girl. We became friends. And we stayed with my grandfather who shared the house with the rabbi of the town. The rabbi wouldn't share it with anyone except my grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the rabbi's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rebbe [? Moishele. ?] That's all I remember. That's-- that's no family name. I was introduced to him. And it-- I remember nights the Hasidim would sit together, dance, and talk and sing. It was like a men's club. It was funny.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Why was it funny?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was funny to me because my parents never separated whether to go to temple or, or anywhere else. And over there, it was men, only the men. And the women's job was to take care of the families. Of course they had gatherings where they were together. But this was kind of a men's thing that they did. And being that I had so much time-- I didn't go to school. I wasn't allowed to because we were illegally there. My father wasn't allowed to work. We were non-existent. And we acquired a title we're never had before. We were refugees.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: In with my grandparents. We were supported by them. There was something talked about sending, from Vienna, my grandmother-- something that was ours. I don't know whether it-- it must have been money. It must have been money. It never got there because the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big a place did your grandparents live in?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were several rooms, very large rooms. Very modestly furnished. It was a far cry from what I was used to. Only I make this comment because I think of it now because it didn't matter to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you-- were put in the room?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- we had the bedroom. We had the bedroom, a whole room that was given to us. My grandparents had a bedroom. And then there was the, the living room and the kitchen. And the schissel where my grandfather washed his hands every half an hour. [LAUGHS] He prayed a lot. He slept, I think, once or twice a week on a hard bench. That was for olam haba, which is for [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] next world, to repent. In order not to be too comfortable on this earth, not to indulge. He was a man so religious, he worried about everyone in town that did not have-- not only himself. My grandmother used to say, he worries more about everybody else than about us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you had some Hebrew education before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. I had-- my father educated me. I did not go to a formal Hebrew school. But in Vienna, every night, I was sitting with a book, the Bible, or-- and translated to me word by word because my father knew a lot. He remembered-- he could quote everything from the Bible and know what it meant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your grandfather's style of praying the same as you were familiar with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. It's the Ashkenazi style of prayer.

speaker: interviewer
text: He-- once a week he had a mission. And I remember once sitting in the room very forlorn and lonely and thinking about Vienna and my grandmother, who I missed so terribly. And he came over and he said, do you know what? I would like to take you on a little walk, for a walk. And I said, OK. Mom, may I go? And she said, sure. And he took me on a tour where he came in to a man-- I just give you an example of one house.

speaker: interviewee
text: The maid opened the door, greeted my grandfather. And the man was very cordial and polite. And he was happy to see my grandfather. I was introduced. Monies were exchanged. He gave my grandfather money. My grandfather wrote it down on a list. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He didn't say thank you. He left. And I have noticed all this because that was my job now. I had nothing else to do but learn and observe. And we went to several places and the same thing happened. I was a little bit intimidated and uncomfortable with this. And when we left, my grandfather gave me a lesson. He says, when you say thank you, you take the good deed away from that person. What I did hear will remain between us. You're not to tell what we did because I never tell anyone about this. There are people that are very needy in this town. And everyone should contribute to their welfare. I collect this money. It goes to the rabbi. And no one knows who gave. And no one knows who received. And that's how it should say-- stay. He-- I said, you didn't say thank you. He said because that would take away from the mitzvah from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do with the list?

speaker: interviewee
text: The list goes with the money to the rabbi. The rabbi knows who contributed. And the money would go to children who needed education, needed pay rent, needed for Shabbos, needed shoes. And that was the social work of my grandfather. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay with your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed to Siberia, all the way through.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We ran away together.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that come about?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war with Poland started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rozwadow was a town that had a train station. It was bombarded a lot. And there was a lot of sirens and bombardment. And we had to constantly hide in the cellar. So they decided that we should go to a town nearby which was called Zaklikow. I remember that. That was a vacation resort, I believe. And the only thing there-- the trait was lumber. It was surrounded by woods, forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everyone packed packages in-- no suitcases, but sheets, wrapped the clothes in the sheet. And you put the sheets on your back and you walk. That is a very innovative way of packing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we walked through the woods at night and day. It was supposed to be a short trip. But interrupted by German airplanes all the time, where we had to hide, get off the road, and sit in the woods because they would shoot at anything that moved. We finally arrived in Zaklikow. And that too was a relative of my father. All I remember her name was Roise. But beyond that, I don't remember. She had-- she-- I think she ran a vacation home. Therefore, there were rooms available for us. And that was a Friday. I don't know how long-- how many days. I think we had a few days kind of very quiet. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One Friday, after the cholent was in the oven, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 25 German airplanes came to surround it and circle the town, burned it from all four corners. First fire bombs, then ran through the town with machine guns and bombs. A shrapnel [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shook up the house that we were in. And dust was coming down. And we couldn't breathe. And I don't know why and how I had done that, but I ran to the kitchen and towels and all kinds of rags with water. And I ran back to my family. And they held it to their mouths so they could breathe. And, therefore, we could think what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ran back where? Where were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They in the room. And I ran to the kitchen for water. We ran out-- we-- my father and mother said out, let's get out. We went out of the building. We ran out of the building. And the airplanes are shooting at everybody. And people are lining the streets. And everyone is running. One is shot dead. And one-- one misses the bullet and keeps on running. And all of us are running. I remember a cow that, from a shrapnel, her intestines were just hanging out. She mooed and fell down. People were walking, running, screaming, falling. They didn't know where to go. It didn't make any difference. We ran. I held on to my father's pants. My sister was in his arms. And he was bending over her in case of a bullet. And all of a sudden, my mother wasn't there. And we came to a place where there were-- it was a lawn-- empty. And there were two trees. And we aimed toward the tree. And there were people underneath the tree. And when my father ran and-- they said something awful to him. Because he picked up and we ran again. And da-da-da-da-da, the machine guns all over us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they say to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I asked him later. And he said that they don't want Jews under the same tree with them because that's how they're going to get killed. And that when they will get rid of all the Jews from Poland, there will be peace. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My father spoke Polish. Of course, I didn't understand it. But he told me. I asked him what they had said. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After-- I don't know how long this bombardment lasted. It was awful. Then it was quiet. It was so quiet. Only here and there like a little explosion going on. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we didn't know. It was like everyone was in shock. And we started, after a while, seeing people crawl from underneath stones, trees, bushes, everywhere, and heading toward town. The town was wiped out. And it was getting dark. We huddled in a corner. But my mother wasn't there. I thought, I'll never see her. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We slept all night huddled in that corner of that ruin. And as soon as daylight began, my father told us not to move. And my uncle went with him because my father didn't function very well. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They found my mother. She was in the woods together with another group of people. She too thought that she lost us. It was a reunion.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean your father didn't function well?

speaker: interviewee
text: He did not function well. He did not. I can't-- it, it-- he was not capable of-- since what they did to him, making decisions fast or-- he was emotionally and mentally less than when he went to Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he feed the baby during the night?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did we feed her?

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- the baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did we feed her? My uncle when, he got some water. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's all, water. We all had water. She cried and she fell asleep. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Oddly enough, the next morning, when we returned to the building that was demolished, the oven with the cholent was intact. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We ate.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] We ate the cholent and started going back to Rozwadow. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came back and planned to escape again because the Germans were coming close. Again, we weren't fast enough. They came to town. And as we were packing, again, banging on the door. I'll never forget the banging. Banging on the door. Raus, raus! [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Good thing that we had started packing. So we took those little bundles of sheets with us. And we go. And we open the door. And the whole town is with SS soldiers on each side with rifles. And they pushing and shoving and beating whoever couldn't. I looked around. All of a sudden, I see the man, the first that gave my grandfather the donation. He was a portly man. And it was hard for him to walk. And the Germans weren't satisfied. So they, with the back of the rifle, they gave them a push. And he fell.

speaker: interviewer
text: And two men picked him up and helped him walk. My grandfather wanted to do it. And they said to him, don't, don't. And I remember another thing, how he said to my grandfather like that, no? Meaning what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meaning, where is God? We all felt it except for my grandfather. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We came to-- to the San, which was the river that they made us cross. They chased us out from town. Everyone was asking where the rabbi is. And no one knew. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We found out later that whatever they were asking of him, he did not do. He, in his tallis, they burned him alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they asking of him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Maybe to deliver Jews or say where the money is or who is rich and who is poor. I don't know. He would not give them what they wanted. And they burned him alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find out that they burned him alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we found out before we came to the Russian border. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We continued. They-- whoever didn't make the boat, was throwing-- thrown into the water. I couldn't swim. I was terrified. I closed my eyes. I didn't want to see what was happening. Someone took my hand and said, jump. And I jumped. And I made the boat. And I opened my eyes and my family was there. We crossed the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a boat are you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not border. We crossed the water. I'm sorry. We crossed the water. And on the other side, there was like--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you cross? You said you jumped on a boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that boat, with the boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of boat was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a little canoe like-- there were a lot of canoes. That was the crossing. And whoever couldn't swim, I assume some people drowned. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] On the other side was like no man's land. There was no Polish government. And the Germans had not reached it. So we were in danger there too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What town was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. It was across the San from Rozwadow. There were towns like Tarnow and [NON-ENGLISH]. But they don't-- I did not speak Polish. And the towns did not mean that much to me because only the events, only peace, only my family. And I saw either the cruelties or kindnesses of people. We started, again, walking. We walked to a town where we were let in to sleep. And that was someone my grandparents knew. They were supposed to leave to Russia. At night, a man came to steal something and shot their daughter dead. That was a Polish man. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Again, tragedy. And we left. The next day, again, into-- through the woods and kept on going. And at one time-- we used to hide when we saw someone on the road before or after us-- but this was a truck. And we were too late. We couldn't escape. And those were about five, six men in their 30s-- 20s and 30s. I found out later that they were Ukrainian men. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They jumped off. They stopped the truck, jumped off, and started talking to my grandfather, my father, all the men. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then they said, you're out of luck because we're going to kill you all. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] were numb. But there was no screaming. There was no-- only huddling, my mother and my father. And my grandfather said, let them go. Take me. And my father said, no. Let them go. Take me. Let my father go. Let them go. I'll give you anything you want. He says, whatever I have is yours. Let us-- let them go. He said, I'll tell you what, whoever misses the bullet will be lucky. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They lined us up. I closed my eyes. [SOBBING] And shots rang out. [SOBBING] I was afraid to open my eyes. I just heard their laughter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Laughing in the truck, their motor started, and they drove away. I knew I was alive. And I don't hear anything. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I opened my eyes. We were all alive. They shot in the air. Their sadistic hunger was satisfied with just that. They had fun. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we continued towards the Russian border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your father or mother talking about the decision why they were going to Russia as opposed to staying under the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, being that we encountered already Germans for a year in Vienna, we knew that it cannot be. We can't survive. They will not let us. And we actually told everybody to run away to Russia. I wish more men, more people, would have listened. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We had another incident on the way to, to the Russian border. We came to a town that was also very quiet. It was toward evening. And we were looking for lodging. And we saw this house with the mezuzah on it. So we knew it's a Jewish house. And we knocked on the door and there was no answer. We tried the door knob. And the door was open.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we knocked again. There's no answer. And we walked in. We have to stop now.
